ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cheese food product class; no specific Turkish white cheese (beyaz peynir) class exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cheese as a broad category. No specific Turkish beyaz peynir code exists; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of cheese as a side dish or meze is approximately 50 g, equivalent to one or two slices or a small portion on a shared platter.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese, feta — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy, FDC ID 173420
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Turkish beyaz peynir (white cheese) is closely analogous to feta cheese in composition — both are brined white cheeses made from sheep or cow milk with similar fat, protein, and sodium profiles. USDA SR Legacy feta (FDC ID 173420) is used as the best available approved-source proxy. Values are per 100 g as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish beyaz peynir. Feta and similar brined white cheeses are classified as low-GI dairy foods. A GI of approximately 27 is used based on the GI value for full-fat cheese reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for cheese. GL per 50 g serving = (27 × 4.09 × 50/100) / 100 ≈ 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (1116 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit intake of brined white cheese such as beyaz peynir due to its exceptionally high sodium content of 1116 mg per 100 g; a 50 g serving contributes approximately 558 mg sodium, representing nearly 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ≈ 27) and minimal carbohydrate content (4.09 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cheese has a very low glycemic index of approximately 27 and contains only 4.09 g of carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a suitable food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; however, the high saturated fat content warrants attention in the context of overall dietary fat management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (14.95 g per 100 g) and high sodium (1116 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brined white cheese is high in saturated fat (approximately 15 g per 100 g) and sodium, both of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this food sparingly and prefer lower-sodium, lower-fat dairy alternatives where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turkish cheese, most commonly beyaz peynir (white cheese), is a brined, semi-soft cheese made from sheep, goat, or cow milk that is central to Turkish cuisine and broader Middle Eastern and Mediterranean food traditions. It is produced by coagulating milk with rennet, pressing the curds, and then storing the cheese in brine, which gives it a distinctly salty, tangy flavour and a crumbly to semi-firm texture. In Turkish restaurants, cheese is typically served as part of a meze spread, alongside olives, tomatoes, cucumbers, and flatbread, or as an accompaniment to grilled meats and kebabs. Nutritionally, beyaz peynir is a rich source of protein (approximately 14 g per 100 g), calcium (approximately 493 mg per 100 g), and fat (approximately 21 g per 100 g), with a high saturated fat content. Its sodium content is exceptionally high at over 1100 mg per 100 g due to the brining process, making it a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its glycemic index is very low (approximately 27), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. The food appears on the field survey data menu of Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine at Boat Quay, where it is offered as a standalone cheese dish or side.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in feta cheese?
Is Turkish white cheese high in fat?
What cheese is used in Turkish cuisine?
Is beyaz peynir the same as feta?
Can I eat cheese if I have high blood pressure?
What are the nutrients in white cheese?

confidence_reason: The food record from field survey data identifies the item only as 'Cheese' within a Turkish restaurant context. Turkish beyaz peynir (white cheese) is the most plausible canonical identity. Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy feta cheese (FDC ID 173420), which is the closest approved-source analogue for a brined white cheese. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact cheese variety served at Sultan Palace is not confirmed, and feta is used as a proxy.
notes: 1. The input food name '92. Cheese' is a menu item number and name from field survey data. The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Cheese (Turkish Restaurant Style)' to reflect the restaurant context while remaining descriptive. 2. Turkish beyaz peynir is the most likely cheese served in a Turkish restaurant meze context; USDA SR Legacy feta (FDC ID 173420) is used as the closest approved-source analogue. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for feta cheese. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for feta cheese; cheese contains no dietary fibre by composition. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for feta cheese. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173420 and are not sentinel -1 values. 5. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables for full-fat cheese; no direct GI measurement for beyaz peynir was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not populated because this is a Turkish cuisine item, not a Singaporean dish; the mandatory multilingual rules apply to Singaporean dishes only. The Turkish romanized name 'Peynir' (and 'Beyaz peynir') is provided in romanized_local and aliases. 7. Price data from field survey (SGD 20.90) is noted but not included in the nutrient record as it is not a nutritional field.
